summary: Name shared by a succession of distinct European polities in eastern France, from a post-Roman Germanic kingdom (5th-6th century, ruled by Gundobad and Godomar) through a medieval Kingdom of Burgundy/Arles within the Holy Roman Empire to, most prominently, the Valois Duchy of Burgundy (1363-1477) — a virtually independent, wine-producing state ruled in succession by Philip the Bold, John the Fearless, Philip the Good, and Charles the Bold. The Valois dukes built one of Europe's wealthiest courts, founding the Order of the Golden Fleece, patronizing painters like Jan van Eyck and the Burgundian School of composers, and repeatedly switching sides in the Hundred Years' War and the Armagnac-Burgundian Civil War. The duchy's independence ended when Charles the Bold died at the 1477 Battle of Nancy; his daughter Mary of Burgundy's marriage to Maximilian I brought its Low Countries lands into the Habsburg dynasty, while the French heartland reverted to the crown. Today "Burgundy" (Bourgogne) names a wine- and food-famous administrative region of east-central France, historically capitaled at Dijon.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How "Burgundy" is identified in giveaways: a name given to several different states in eastern France across history, synonymous with a dark red/purple color from its namesake wine, and most famously the virtually independent Valois duchy ruled by John the Fearless, Philip the Good, and Charles the Bold, which often switched sides during the Hundred Years' War.

clues.clue: The name is synonymous with a dark red/purple color, associated with its namesake wine
examples: what name given to many different states in eastern France is synonymous with a dark shade of red associated with its wine?
Philip the Good and Charles the Bold ruled what duchy whose wine production inspired the name of a dark red-purple color?

clues.clue: Ruled in the 15th century by a cadet branch of the Valois dynasty under dukes John the Fearless, Philip the Good, and Charles the Bold, who often changed sides during the Hundred Years' War
examples: A cadet branch of the Valois dynasty ruled a virtually-independent duchy with this name that often changed sides during the Hundred Years' War under dukes like Philip the Good and Charles the Bold.
name this region in France ruled by John the Fearless, Philip the Good, and Charles the Bold.

clues.clue: A dynasty with this name was first to use a cross of five blue shields with white dots, a design still used on Portugal's flag
examples: A dynasty with this name was the first to use a cross made of five blue shields with white dots that is still used on Portugal's flag.

works.name: Early Burgundian Kingdoms
works.description: Before the Valois duchy, the name belonged to a post-Roman Germanic kingdom founded around Worms in the 5th century and, later, a medieval Kingdom of Burgundy (also called Arles/Arelat) within the Holy Roman Empire — historian Norman Davies counted fifteen distinct European polities to bear the name.

clues.clue: Took its name from a post-Roman Germanic people led by Godomar and Gundobad, the latter of whom issued the namesake law code (the Lex Burgundionum/Gundobada) in the 5th century CE
examples: This region got its name from barbarians led by Godomar and Gundobad, the latter of whom issued its namesake law code in the 5th century CE, and it organized the Order of the Golden Fleece.
A kingdom of this name experienced a civil war between Godegesil and his brother, a king who created his namesake law code, the Lex Gundobada.

clues.clue: This early kingdom was the site of a civil war between King Gundobad and his brother Godegesil
examples: A kingdom of this name experienced a civil war between Godegesil and his brother, a king who created his namesake law code, the Lex Gundobada.

clues.clue: Historian Norman Davies identified fifteen European polities to bear this name; the first was established under King Gunther at Worms in the 5th century
examples: Norman Davies identified fifteen European polities which had this name at different times; the first was established under King Gunther at Worms in the fifth century, while a later one formed from part of Middle Francia had its capital at Arles.

clues.clue: An upper and lower state with this name were later merged into a kingdom sometimes called Arelat within the Holy Roman Empire, formed in part from Middle Francia and capitaled at Arles
examples: An upper and lower state with this name were merged to form a kingdom sometimes called Arelat within the Holy Roman Empire.

clues.clue: Queen Balthild ruled a united Austrasia, Neustria, and a kingdom with this name after the death of her husband Clovis II; this kingdom was later divided between East and West Francia by the Treaty of Meerssen
examples: Queen Balthild later ruled a united Austrasia, Neustria, and this kingdom to the south after the death of her husband Clovis II. This kingdom was later divided between East and West Francia by the Treaty of Meerssen.

clues.clue: For several hundred years, part of the state's lands formed a "Free County" (Franche-Comte) due to rights won by ruler Reginald III; two places with the name agreed to mutual neutrality in 1522 at Saint-Jean-de-Losne
examples: For several hundred years, part of this state's lands were a "free county" due to rights won by its ruler Reginald III.
Two places with this same name agreed to be neutral in 1522 at Saint-Jean-de-Losne.

works.name: Philip the Bold and the Founding of the Valois Duchy
works.description: The Valois Duchy of Burgundy was created in 1363 when King John II of France granted it to his younger son, Philip the Bold, beginning nearly 150 years of Valois rule that later Romantic historians (per Johan Huizinga) mythologized as an "overripe" chivalric court culture.

clues.clue: The duchy was created in 1363 by King John II of France for his son Philip the Bold; 20th-century historian Johan Huizinga wrote of the enduring myth of chivalry surrounding its founding
examples: Huizinga wrote of the enduring myth of chivalry being instrumental in the creation of this duchy by John II.
In his book The Autumn of the Middle Ages, Johan Huizinga argues a state with this name made of patchwork fiefdoms sponsored an "overripe" chivalric court culture that awaited the Renaissance.

clues.clue: As regent for young King Charles VI, dismissed a group of royal councilors nicknamed the "Marmosets"; the duchy had earlier been occupied by the mercenary captain Arnaut de Cervole, nicknamed the "Archpriest"
examples: One ruler of this polity dismissed a group of advisers named the Marmosets in order to secure a regency, and it was once occupied by a highwayman nicknamed the "Archpriest," Arnaut de Cervole.

clues.clue: His personal motto "il me tarde" ("I look forward") appeared on his seal; he protected Pierre de Créon after Créon stole 100,000 francs from the Duke of Anjou
examples: One lord of this polity, whose personal motto il me tarde or "I look forward" was on his seal, protected Pierre de Creon after he had stolen 100,000 francs from the Duc de Anjou.

clues.clue: Ordered the construction of a Carthusian monastery, the Chartreuse de Champmol, as a burial site for his dynasty; sculptor Claus Sluter's Well of Moses was created for its cloister
examples: A ruler with this title ordered the construction of a Carthusian monastery as a burial site for his dynasty.
Claus Sluter sculpted the Well of Moses for this region's duke John the Fearless. The Chartreuse de Champmol was constructed as tomb for its ruler Philip the Bold.

clues.clue: Arranged a 1385 double wedding at Cambrai in which a prince and princess from Burgundy married a princess and prince from Bavaria
examples: A prince and princess from this region married a princess and a prince from Bavaria in a double wedding held at Cambrai.

works.name: John the Fearless
works.description: Philip the Bold's son and successor, whose 1407 assassination of his cousin Louis of Orléans plunged France into the Armagnac-Burgundian Civil War; he seized Paris after Agincourt before being murdered in revenge in 1419.

clues.clue: Had his cousin Louis of Orléans hacked to death by street thugs, then allegedly stole his mistress, Queen Isabeau
examples: Another had his cousin Louis of Orleans hacked to death by street thugs and then stole his mistress Isabeau.

The Holy Roman Empire contained lands split off from this title known as the "Free County." A holder of the title started a civil war after assassinating the Duke of Orleans and vied for the French throne with England and his mentally handicapped cousin Charles VI.

clues.clue: Enlisted theologian Jean Petit to publicly defend the assassination he had perpetrated, which triggered the Cabochien Revolt
examples: A holder of this position enlisted the theologian Jean Petit to defend an assassination that he had perpetrated, which triggered the Cabochien revolt.

clues.clue: Fought the Armagnac faction, led by Bernard VII of Armagnac, in the civil war sparked by the assassination of Louis I of Orléans
examples: A group led by Bernard VII of Armagnac fought holders of this position during a civil war started by the assassination of Louis I of Orleans.

clues.clue: Seized Paris after the Battle of Agincourt
examples: That man, John the Fearless, had seized Paris after Agincourt.

clues.clue: Murdered on the bridge at Montereau by agents of the Dauphin (future Charles VII) in 1419, in revenge for the Orléans assassination
examples: Its monarch was murdered on the Montereau bridge by agents of the Dauphin in 1419.

Many of the surviving records of animal executions come from the Chambre des comptes of this region of eastern France. Centred on the city of Dijon, its duke John the Fearless was assassinated on the Bridge of Montereau in 1419.

works.name: Philip the Good (Philip III)
works.description: John the Fearless's son, whose long reign (1419-1467) marked the height of Burgundian wealth and prestige: he founded the Order of the Golden Fleece, patronized Jan van Eyck and Guillaume Dufay, captured Joan of Arc, and briefly reallied with France at the Peace of Arras.

clues.clue: Founded the Order of the Golden Fleece to celebrate his marriage to Isabella of Portugal
examples: The creation of the Order of the Golden Fleece celebrated the wedding of a ruler of this state to Isabella of Portugal.
The Order of the Golden Fleece was founded by Philip the Good, who built up the power of this duchy also ruled by Charles the Bold and John the Fearless.

clues.clue: Hosted the 1454 Feast of the Pheasant, a lavish banquet meant to drum up support for a crusade against the Ottomans after their capture of Constantinople, though no crusade ever took place
examples: Name this duke of Burgundy who hosted the Feast of the Pheasant. This father of Charles the Bold founded the Order of the Golden Fleece.
Philip the Good organized this event to promote a planned crusade in response to the Ottoman sack of Constantinople.

clues.clue: Patronized Jan van Eyck from 1425 until the artist's death, as well as composer Guillaume Dufay
examples: That ruler with this title patronized Jan van Eyck from 1425 until his death.
Such luminaries as Guilliame Dufay and Jan van Eyck were artists in this man's court.

clues.clue: Founded the Hospices de Beaune, an almshouse in the region's namesake Romanesque style, along with his Chancellor Nicolas Rolin
examples: This state's namesake Romanesque style of architecture is exemplified by the Hospices de Beaune, an almshouse founded by this state's Chancellor Nicolas Rolin.

clues.clue: Won the last Hook and Cod War against Jacoba (Jacqueline) of Bavaria/Hainaut, who ceded three counties to him
examples: Jacoba of Bavaria ceded three counties to this ruler after losing the very last Hook and Cod War to him.
In a phase of the Hook and Cod wars, the conservative Hooks supported Jacqueline of Bavaria while the Cods supported a ruler of this region.

clues.clue: His forces captured Joan of Arc at Compiègne and handed her to his English allies, who put her on heresy trial
examples: In the campaign against Compeigne, his troops captured Joan of Arc and handed her to his English allies.

Yolande was an early supporter of the Armagnacs, who opposed a faction led by this French duchy. Under the leadership of Philip the Good, this duchy captured Joan of Arc and turned her over to the English to be executed.

clues.clue: Signed the 1435 Peace/Treaty of Arras, reallying with France's Charles VII and ending the Armagnac-Burgundian Civil War; his later support of the Praguerie revolt ended this short alliance
examples: Another lord of this polity, who signed the Peace of Arras in alliance with France in 1435, was Philip the Good.
The support of the Praguerie by this son of John the Fearless ended a short alliance that he forged with Charles VII via the Treaty of Arras.

clues.clue: Farmed out a substantial portion of his tax income to the Italian cloth merchant Giovanni Arnolfini
examples: Another ruler with this title farmed out a substantial portion of his tax income to the Italian cloth merchant Giovanni Arnolfini.

clues.clue: Regulated the quality of Burgundy's namesake dark red wine
examples: This French region's duke Philip the Good regulated the quality of its namesake dark red wine.

works.name: Charles the Bold
works.description: The last Valois Duke of Burgundy, an ambitious and combative ruler whose feud with Louis XI of France and wars against the Swiss ended in his death at the 1477 Battle of Nancy, dissolving the independent duchy.

clues.clue: Nicknamed "the Bold" (le Téméraire, more literally "the Rash")
examples: One duke of this region's nickname can be translated as "rash," but he is normally spoken of in historical records as "Charles the Bold."
Name this nobleman from the House of Valois whose death at the Battle of Nancy resulted in the dissolution of his former territory.

clues.clue: Refused to shave after losing a battle in which the "Three Brothers" jewelry was stolen from his tent
examples: A ruler of this state refused to shave after he lost a battle during which the Three Brothers jewelry was stolen from his tent.

clues.clue: As Count of Charolais, formed the League of the Public Weal against Louis XI, with Charles, Duke of Berry, serving as its nominal figurehead
examples: One duke of this place was count of Charolais while he appointed his brother to figurehead the League of the Public Weal.
Although Charles, Duke of Berry, nominally led this alliance, its real leader was Charles the Bold. This group of French nobles rose up against the power of Louis XI.

clues.clue: After defeating Louis XI at Montlhéry (1465), forced him to sign the Treaty of Conflans, granting control of Boulogne, Ponthieu, and Guînes; this dissolved the League of the Public Weal
examples: After winning at Montlhery in 1465, Charles forced Louis to sign this treaty giving him control of Boulogne, Ponthieu, and Guines. It led to the dissolution of the League of the Public Weal.

clues.clue: Put down several rebellions by the city of Liège and allied with England by marrying Margaret of York
examples: A leader of this region put down several rebellions by the city of Liege and forged an alliance with England by marrying Margaret of York.

clues.clue: Funded Edward IV's army before the Battle of Barnet as part of an English alliance; the Earl of Warwick partly broke with Edward IV over this Burgundian alliance
examples: The Earl of Warwick partly broke with Edward IV over Edward's decision to seek an alliance with this duchy. Prior to the Battle of Barnet, Edward IV raised an army with money from its duke Charles the Bold.

clues.clue: Paid 300,000 florins to subsume the lands of Arnold of Egmont, Duke of Guelders, absorbing the territory after Arnold's death
examples: One polity of this name paid 300,000 florins to subsume the lands of Arnold of Egmont, Duke of Guelders, and absorbed that land after Arnold's death.

clues.clue: Died fighting Swiss and Lorraine forces allied with Louis XI (the "Spider-King") at the 1477 Battle of Nancy, ending independent Burgundian statehood; the duchy at its height had claimed land as far north as Flanders
examples: This duchy's power faded at Nancy in 1477, when Charles the Bold died in battle against forces of the "Spider-King," Louis XI.
It was dissolved after its leader was killed in the snow at Nancy.

works.name: Mary of Burgundy and the Habsburg Succession
works.description: Charles the Bold's only child and heir, whose 1477 marriage to Holy Roman Emperor Maximilian I brought the Burgundian Low Countries into the Habsburg dynasty (giving rise to the saying "happy Austria, marry"), while the French heartland of the duchy reverted to Louis XI's crown.

clues.clue: Inherited the Burgundian title after her father Charles the Bold's death at Nancy, signing the "Great Privilege" restoring regional freedoms and abolishing the Parliament of Mechelen, after her succession was affirmed by the Joyous Entry of 1477
examples: This ruler preserved regional freedoms and abolished the Parliament of Mechelen upon signing the "Great Privilege."
One of this state's princesses signed the "Great Privilege" restoring the rights of cities after her succession was affirmed by the Joyous Entry of 1477.

clues.clue: Married Holy Roman Emperor Maximilian I as his first wife; under his father Frederick III, the Habsburgs coined the saying "Let others wage war; you, happy Austria, marry" from this marriage
examples: Under its scion Frederick III, the Habsburgs gained the saying "Let others wage war; you, happy Austria, marry" due to the marriage of his son, Maximilian I, to Mary the Rich, heiress of this region.
Mary the Rich inherited this title after the death of another holder in the battle of Nancy, which led to Habsburg rule of the Low Countries.

clues.clue: Died in a riding accident; legendarily reappeared as a shade summoned by Johannes Trithemius in a Faust-like episode; Maximilian fictionalized their courtship as a chivalric journey in the poem Theuerdank
examples: After dying in an accident, this ruler legendarily reappeared as a shade when summoned by Johannes Trithemius in a Faust-like episode.
This ruler's spouse fictionalized a chivalric journey to marry this ruler in the Arthurian-inspired poem Theuerdank.

clues.clue: Margaret of York displayed Mary's infant son (Philip the Handsome) naked to a crowd to disprove rumors, spread by Louis XI (the "Universal Spider"), that the child was a girl
examples: Margaret of York displayed this woman's infant son naked to a crowd in order to disprove rumors, spread by a king dubbed the "Universal Spider," that the boy was a girl.

clues.clue: The Treaty of Senlis settled the war over the Burgundian succession, ending hostilities over the Seventeen Provinces and confirming Mary's inheritance; the Spanish Habsburgs later flew a "Cross of Burgundy" flag (a red X on white)
examples: The Spanish Habsburgs used a flag of a red "X" shape on a white background called this state's "Cross." The war of succession over its lands was settled by the Treaty of Senlis, which concluded hostilities over the Seventeen Provinces and determined Mary the Rich's inheritance.

clues.clue: By the 1482 Treaty of Arras, Maximilian I gained the County of Flanders from the duchy, while the rest went to Louis XI's France
examples: By the Treaty of Arras, Maximilian I gained the County of Flanders from this duchy, while the rest went to Louis XI's France.

works.name: Burgundian State Institutions
works.description: The Valois duchy left an administrative legacy within the Holy Roman Empire: its lands were later organized as the Burgundian Circle, and its heraldic emblems long outlived Valois rule.

clues.clue: The duchy's lands were later organized as the "Burgundian Circle" within the Holy Roman Empire, which included the "Generality Lands" pacified by the Guelders Wars
examples: In the Holy Roman Empire, a "circle" of realms named for this state included the "Generality Lands" pacified by the Guelders Wars.

works.name: Burgundian Art, Music, and Illuminated Manuscripts
works.description: The Valois ducal court, especially under Philip the Good, made Burgundy one of Europe's great artistic centers: the Burgundian School of composers pioneered polyphonic techniques, while painters and sculptors including Jan van Eyck, Rogier van der Weyden, and Claus Sluter worked for the dukes.

clues.clue: The Burgundian School of composers, centered at Philip the Good's court, included Guillaume Dufay and Gilles Binchois, and later Antoine Busnois; the school is known for polyphony and the technique of faux-bourdon (parallel first-inversion triads), partly inspired by the intervals in John Dunstaple's works
examples: Name this polity. Gilles Binchois and Guillaume Dufay were representatives of a school of composers named for this polity.
Binchois, like Guillaume Du Fay, was a member of this 15th-century school of composition centered on the court of Philip the Good.

clues.clue: Composers wrote musical settings of L'homme armé, a favorite song of a Burgundian duke, possibly prompted by his call for a crusade at the Feast of the Pheasant
examples: Musical settings of L'homme arme, the favorite song of a ruler with this title, may have been prompted by a call for a crusade by a ruler with this title that was promoted by the Feast of the Pheasant.

clues.clue: Painters including Jan van Eyck and Rogier van der Weyden flourished at the ducal court, often centered at Brussels
examples: Painters like van der Weyden flourished at the court of one of these rulers, often centered at Brussels.
That ruler with this title patronized Jan van Eyck from 1425 until his death.

clues.clue: Sculptor Claus Sluter created the Well of Moses for the Chartreuse de Champmol under John the Fearless; sculptor Jean de la Huerta created miniature mourner statues for ducal tombs
examples: Claus Sluter sculpted the Well of Moses for this region's duke John the Fearless.
Miniature statures of mourners were created for the tombs of rulers of this title by sculptors like Jean de la Huerta.

works.name: Modern Region of Burgundy (Bourgogne)
works.description: Today "Burgundy" (Bourgogne) names an administrative and cultural region of east-central France, historically capitaled at Dijon and famous worldwide for its wine and hearty regional cuisine.

clues.clue: Historic capital is Dijon; in the 2010s the region was merged with its former "Free County," Franche-Comté, to form the official region of Bourgogne-Franche-Comté; its "climats" and "terroirs" (monastery-developed wine-growing plots) comprise a UNESCO World Heritage Site
examples: In the 2010s, this area was reunited with Franche-Comte, its free county, to form a new official region.
The "climats" and "terroirs" of this region comprise a UNESCO World Heritage Site, reflecting the development of agricultural practices in monasteries around its historical capital of Dijon.

clues.clue: Names a beef stew (boeuf bourguignon) made with carrots, onions, mushrooms, and the region's namesake dry red wine
examples: This region names a type of beef stew made with carrots, onions, and mushrooms, sometimes made with a sauce using this region's namesake dry, red wine.

Further north and west of Savoy in eastern France is this historical region, whose major towns include Dijon and Auxerre, and which gives the French version of its name to a type of beef casserole cooked with red wine.

clues.clue: Parsleyed ham (jambon persillé), an Easter dish, is prepared by setting cubed pork shoulder and calf's-foot gelatin in a terrine
examples: An Easter dish from this region, sometimes prepared by setting cubed pork shoulder and calf's feet gelatin in a terrine, is called parsleyed ham.

clues.clue: The Kir cocktail, a mix of white wine and local blackcurrant liqueur (crème de cassis), was popularized by Félix Kir, mayor of Dijon
examples: Felix Kir, the mayor of a city in this region, popularized a mixed drink made with local blackcurrant liqueur.

clues.clue: The soft cheese Époisses is made in its namesake town in the Côte-d'Or department; major cities include Beaune and Auxerre, and the region is home to the Arboretum de Pézanin
examples: The soft cheese Epoisse is made in a namesake town in the department of Cote-d'Or in this region. Major cities in this region include Beaune and Auxerre, and it is home to the spectacular Arboretum de Pezanin.

comprehensive_summary: "Burgundy" has named a succession of distinct European polities across more than a millennium. The earliest was a post-Roman Germanic kingdom, established by King Gunther at Worms in the 5th century and later ruled by Gundobad, who issued the namesake Lex Burgundionum (Lex Gundobada) law code amid a civil war with his brother Godegesil; a later medieval Kingdom of Burgundy (sometimes called Arles/Arelat) formed from Middle Francia within the Holy Roman Empire, while Queen Balthild once ruled a united Austrasia, Neustria, and Burgundy before it was divided by the Treaty of Meerssen. The name's most famous incarnation is the Valois Duchy of Burgundy, created in 1363 when King John II of France granted the territory to his son Philip the Bold, who built the Chartreuse de Champmol (with Claus Sluter's Well of Moses) as a dynastic burial site and arranged a 1385 double wedding with Bavaria at Cambrai. His son John the Fearless assassinated his cousin Louis of Orléans in 1407, triggering the Armagnac-Burgundian Civil War and the Cabochien Revolt; John seized Paris after Agincourt before being murdered in revenge on the bridge at Montereau in 1419. John's son Philip the Good presided over the duchy's golden age: he founded the Order of the Golden Fleece upon marrying Isabella of Portugal, hosted the 1454 Feast of the Pheasant to (unsuccessfully) drum up a crusade against the Ottomans, patronized Jan van Eyck and composer Guillaume Dufay, founded the Hospices de Beaune with Chancellor Nicolas Rolin, won the last Hook and Cod War against Jacoba of Bavaria, captured Joan of Arc at Compiègne and handed her to the English, and briefly reallied with France at the 1435 Peace of Arras. His son and successor Charles the Bold, the duchy's last Valois ruler, formed the League of the Public Weal against Louis XI, defeated him at Montlhéry to win the Treaty of Conflans, put down rebellions at Liège, allied with England by marrying Margaret of York and funding Edward IV before the Battle of Barnet, and absorbed the Duchy of Guelders — before dying fighting Swiss and Lorraine forces at the 1477 Battle of Nancy, which ended independent Burgundian statehood. His daughter and heir, Mary of Burgundy, signed the "Great Privilege" restoring regional freedoms and married Holy Roman Emperor Maximilian I (inspiring the Habsburg motto "happy Austria, marry"), before dying in a riding accident; the succession war was settled by the Treaty of Senlis, and the 1482 Treaty of Arras split her inheritance, sending Flanders to Maximilian and the French heartland to Louis XI. Burgundian lands were later organized as the Burgundian Circle of the Holy Roman Empire. The ducal court also fostered the Burgundian School of composers (Dufay, Binchois, Busnois), known for polyphony and faux-bourdon, and employed painters like Jan van Eyck and Rogier van der Weyden and sculptors like Claus Sluter and Jean de la Huerta. Today, "Burgundy" (Bourgogne) survives as a French administrative region historically capitaled at Dijon — merged with Franche-Comté in the 2010s — famous for its namesake wine, boeuf bourguignon, parsleyed ham, the Kir cocktail, and Époisses cheese.
recursive_suggestions: Charles the Bold — recurring near-standalone answerline (3x recurring bonus part; also 'Duke of Burgundy' tossup clues), the duchy's most vivid ruler
Philip the Good / Philip III of Burgundy — has his own tossup answerline (2012 ACF Nationals) and 2x recurring bonus-part presence
John the Fearless — recurring figure central to the Armagnac-Burgundian Civil War; frequently clued across both 'Burgundy' and 'Duke of Burgundy' tossups
Mary of Burgundy — has her own tossup answerline (2026 ACF Nationals); could become standalone with further recurrence
Order of the Golden Fleece — recurring institution (founded by Philip the Good) across many questions; watch for its own answerline
Feast of the Pheasant — recurring bonus-part answerline (2x), a vivid standalone event
Louis XI ("the Spider King") — recurring antagonist throughout the Charles the Bold and Mary of Burgundy material; a major French History topic in his own right

Book of Hours — a 2019 ACF Nationals tossup clues the 'black' Hours of Mary of Burgundy and the Turin-Milan Hours, but its actual answer is the illuminated-manuscript genre 'Book of Hours,' not Burgundy; excluded from this analysis
Baxter — a 2021 MUSES II tossup prompts on "Burgundy" only because the answer, Ron Burgundy's dog from the Anchorman films, shares the surname; entirely unrelated to the historical topic and excluded

Kingdom of Burgundy (5th-6th century Germanic kingdom; later medieval Kingdom of Burgundy/Arles) — historically distinct from the Valois Duchy despite sharing a name; watch for answerline confusion in future clue sets
